synthesis.summary: Alphabet reported a standout earnings quarter that triggered a 7.67% single-day surge, the stock's largest breakout in over two decades. Strong financial results directly drove both the price spike and elevated volume, consistent with a post-earnings gap-up. RSI at 80.4 signals the move has pushed the stock into overbought territory, but the earnings catalyst fully accounts for the magnitude and direction.

Alphabet reported an exceptionally strong quarterly earnings result, triggering a ~7.8% single-day surge on above-average volume — the stock's largest breakout in over two decades. The earnings beat is the direct driver, with RSI at 80 confirming the move pushed GOOGL into overbought territory.

Meta's Q1 earnings contained at least one disclosure — likely around forward capex guidance or cost outlook — that overshadowed otherwise solid results, triggering a sharp selloff. Separately, the macro backdrop worsened the hit: Q1 GDP came in below expectations and the Fed's preferred inflation gauge jumped to 3.2%, reinforcing a stagflationary read that pressures high-multiple tech names disproportionately. The combination of a company-specific earnings concern and a deteriorating macro print accounts for the near-10% drop on elevated volume.

Royal Caribbean reported an earnings beat that exceeded profit expectations, driving the nearly 9% single-day surge. Demand commentary in the report signaled no material softening in cruise bookings, removing a key bear concern and amplifying the upside reaction.

QCOM is up ~19% today on volume more than double its average, but the supplied news contains no Qualcomm-specific catalyst — only a general market round-up covering Caterpillar's earnings and several unrelated names. The move's magnitude and direction are unexplained by available news; likely drivers such as a Qualcomm earnings beat, guidance update, or major partnership announcement are not present in the provided inputs.
